sections.markdown:
# IN-PACKAGE: Switching Mid-File

The **current package** is held in the special variable `*package*`. The reader uses it to resolve unqualified symbol names: when you type `foo`, the reader looks up `"FOO"` in `*package*`, interning if necessary.

`(in-package #:my-app)` mutates `*package*` to point at `MY-APP`. Conventionally every Lisp source file looks like:

```lisp
(defpackage #:my-app
  (:use #:cl)
  (:export #:start))

(in-package #:my-app)

(defun start () ...)
```

The `defpackage` runs in whatever package the loader started in (usually `cl-user`); `in-package` then switches so that everything below it — including new `defun`s — is interned in `MY-APP`.

At the REPL, `(in-package :foo)` lets you experiment from inside a particular package: now unqualified names refer to `FOO`'s symbols, and tab-completion / symbol probing work against `FOO`. To get back, `(in-package :cl-user)`.

sections.explain: At the REPL, *PACKAGE* is normally CL-USER.
Binding *PACKAGE* with LET swaps it locally — the same effect IN-PACKAGE has globally. Note this works because *PACKAGE* is a special variable.
After the LET, *PACKAGE* is restored. The READ-FROM-STRING inside saw TMP2.

sections.prompt: Define CURRENT-PKG-NAME () returning the name of *PACKAGE* as a string. Then define WITH-PKG-NAME (PKGNAME) that creates a package with that name (using CL), executes (current-pkg-name) inside it via LET on *PACKAGE*, and returns the result. Both arguments are strings.
sections.hint: (defun current-pkg-name () (package-name *package*)) and (defun with-pkg-name (pkgname) (unless (find-package pkgname) (make-package pkgname :use '(:cl))) (let ((*package* (find-package pkgname))) (current-pkg-name))).
